occasions whereby sump pumps struggle to keep up with the amount of spillage reporting to them. \nWhether it be due to compromised performance, bogged lines and on many occasions the sumps are not bogged, but the sheer volume of spillage along with the addition of hose water used to enable clean up proves more than the pumps are able to manage hindering the clean-up process.\nAt this stage to This would eliminate the possibility of it being lost, however again could potentially pose a risk of becoming a trip hazard. \nThe most favorable option would be to create a hinged hatch, which can Derreck Screens","IdentifyRectify_BriefDescription":"Improvement suggestion for maintenance on Derreck Screens Sump Pumps","IdentifyRectify_ListExpectedBenefits":"The current practice for the removal and re-installation of the Derreck Screens Sump Pumps presents hazards that have been identified through risk assessments and also through the recordable history of Incidents.  Hazards and Risks such as; personnel exposure to manual handling and potentially double handling, Pinch and Crush Points, Suspended loads and lifting operations, personnel in close proximity to Rigging and Crane Operations potentially uncontrolled movement, and with the restricted head room which subsequently increases the risk of cranage verses structure, which may also result in plant and equipment damage.  Using the hierarchy of controls, to mitigate the risk in addressing harm to personnel, reducing the likelihood of plant and equipment damage, streamlining the efficiency of maintenance for both planned and unplanned plant downtime which will result in a reduction of production loss, I would suggest the implementation of an engineering and design change, with the introduction of mechanical aid removable monorail/gantry.  \n\nIn the photos attached, is the example of the removal of Sump Pump SP610.  For Illustration purposes, notice the franna boom in relation to the structure where the pipe bracing is approximately 150mm.  In this instance, to successfully remove the Sump Pump we were required to install rigging equipment, engaging the use a chain block and lever block to achieve an duel lift, indexing the angle to remove the sump pump, requiring a full length of 2600mm.  This is typical of the 2661 plant area for the Derreck Screens.  This proposal is for the installation of a removable gantry monorail, hanger style, that clamps to the existing overhead structural \"I BEAM\".  This will allow for the increase in allowable head room, due to the removal of the Franna Crane boom, as the monorail will extend out, which will drop below the cleat of the pipe bracing. At the least this is reduce the interaction with the Franna Boom and the structure. \n\nIn addition to mitigating the fore-mentioned hazards and risks, this will also have a lasting affect for reducing the necessity for franna cranage when performing future maintenance works such as decoupling motor to pump, or removing the sump pump for the cleaning of the sump pump bowl.\n\nThis proposal EDWARDS","Key_Employee_TrackerUpdatedBy":null,"Employee_TrackerUpdatedBy_Name":null,"SMEChampion_ProposedResources":null,"SMEChampion_ReviewComments":null,"SMEChampion_ProvideSupportComments":null,"Key_Hazard_Related":null,"IdentifyRectify_SpecificLocation":"Iron Bridge OPF Air classification building","IdentifyRectify_BriefDescription":"Change vibe feeders out  for belt feeders to alleviate slumping issues.","IdentifyRectify_ListExpectedBenefits":"Change vibe feeders out for belt feeders to alleviate slumping issues.... I am not an engineer or metallurgist, but I do have a considerable number of years under my belt as a fixed plant operator and experience in the way product reacts in different circumstances...   As we all know there have been several serious slumping events in the air classification building due to different issues with grind size of product etc. Every time we have a slump it's a time-consuming task to clear the area around CV 403 tail end to re start. A lot of person hours spent on a task that in my opinion could be remedied by replacing the existing vibe feeders with belt feeders. As a slump occurs, the product, being so fine falls down through the bin and fluidizes, then passes directly through the vibe feeder which is at the perfect angle to accommodate a slump, whether the vibe feeder is running or not, acting like a waterfall, which falls directly through to the conveyor below causing serious issues with bogging under the conveyor etc. and at times tripping the belt rip switch at the tail end of the conveyor. If the vibe feeders are replaced with belt feeders any slump would be immediately stopped as soon as the product hits the feeder as it has nowhere else to fall due to the lack of angle of the belt feeder, and the confinement inside the belt feeder housing. This would obviously be a large change out and a large budget, but I believe doing this would alleviate more slumping issues. There would most likely still be slumping in the bins, but it would likely not cause the issues that we have with product fluidizing down onto the conveyor below. It would simply fall onto the belt feeder then stop. Any questions please do not hesitate to contact me. & FS001","IdentifyRectify_BriefDescription":"In a recent incident (INC-109851), it was noted that the fire water backup diesel pump, PU206 had seemingly started by itself, when assumed to be isolated.   \nAfter further investigation, we have confirmed that the only way for this to happen is that the key switch would have to be left in the \"crank normal\" position on the main panel door.\nEven though there is a 230V isolator on the main door, this only is used for the battery chargers.  Even in the off position, the batteries still supply 12VDC and 24VDC to the \ncontrol system.  The key switch has two positions, \"crank isolate\", and \"crank normal\".\n\nWhen the key is in \"crank isolate\" position, it is impossible for the system to start, whether from the START buttons or the PRESSURE SWITCHES that are coded to the PLC\nfor a START on PU206. \n\nWhen the key is in the \"crank normal\" position, either of the START buttons or PRESSURE SWITCHES (PS-0005, 006) will send a signal to the PLC, issuing a start command \nfor the diesel pump.\n\nWith this in mind, we have come up with a couple solutions to prevent this from happening in the future.\n\nThe first option would be to remove the key switch and replace with a simple 2 position selector switch, complete with a see-through lockable cover on the main panel.\n\nThe second option would be to add a lockable battery isolator near the starter of the diesel pump. \n\nBoth options would still leave control power available in the cabinet for trouble shooting purposes and would make it impossible for the diesel pump to start up unintentionally.\n\nPlease see documents attached for further clarification.\n\n","IdentifyRectify_ListExpectedBenefits":"1.
